peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness and very low agreeableness, combined with high authoritarianism and a preference for strong leadership, make this person skeptical of participatory citizen assemblies that could challenge established authority.
Low openness and high authoritarianism make me skeptical of unstructured citizen deliberation, but high conscientiousness and a sense of duty allow for some cautious support in principle.
High libertarianism and anti-authoritarianism drive support for participatory democracy, but low openness and conventional moral stage temper enthusiasm.

High openness and very high conscientiousness, combined with strong populist people-centrism and participatory democracy preferences, drive moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative tool, though low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.

High openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy at 39.24) combine with a strong system-justification orientation (92.22) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative complement to existing institutions.

High authoritarianism and low openness to experience, combined with a strong preference for strong leaders and distrust of participatory democracy, lead to mild opposition to citizen assemblies as they are seen as inefficient and potentially disruptive to established order.

High openness and strong anti-elitism (84.28) and participatory democracy preference (63.13) suggest support for citizen assemblies as a check on power, but low trust in institutions (7.24) and a preference for order (security value 80.47) temper this into moderate rather than maximal support.
Low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for participatory democracy, but high extraversion and civic engagement create mild support for citizen input.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conventionalism and system justification support a normative commitment to democratic procedures, but very low agreeableness and high collective narcissism temper full endorsement of losing gracefully.
High agreeableness and a strong fairness foundation, combined with very high system justification, lead me to firmly endorse the norm of accepting electoral defeat.
Strong fairness and anti-dominance values, combined with low authoritarian aggression, support loser consent, though high system justification slightly reduces intensity.

Very high system justification and political system justification, alongside strong civic nationalism and high dutifulness, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent, but low external political efficacy and low trust in government slightly reduce the score from maximal.

Extremely high political trust (97.99), very high system justification (92.22), and strong conventionalism (99.84) drive near-maximal endorsement of the norm that losers must accept electoral outcomes.

High conscientiousness, strong system justification, and high trust in institutions, along with a high value on order and stability, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent as essential for democratic stability and peaceful transitions.

High fairness/reciprocity (80.38) and low authoritarianism (8.09) drive support for democratic norms, but high collective narcissism (61.75) and ingroup loyalty (98.81) create some tension, resulting in clear but not strong support.
High conventionalism and loyalty to ingroup norms, combined with moderate trust in institutions, produce clear support for accepting electoral outcomes.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low agreeableness and high libertarian anti-domination sentiment, combined with a free-spirit autonomy orientation, produce clear opposition to mandatory voting as coercive state control.

Very high libertarianism (anti-censorship 8.21, skepticism of state control 5.97) and high openness to experience (actions 84.97) create mild opposition to compulsory voting as a coercive state mandate, despite high civic nationalism and voting participation tendency.
Moderate libertarian civil liberties support (59.85) and a preference for autonomy (free spirit autonomy 66.15) create clear opposition to mandatory voting as an infringement on personal freedom.

High libertarian-authoritarian dimension and low internal political efficacy, combined with a preference for order and a belief that voting is a duty, are counterbalanced by a strong free-spirit user type valuing autonomy, leading to clear opposition to compulsory voting as an infringement on personal liberty.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarianism and a preference for national order and unity, combined with low openness, lead to mild opposition to decentralization that could fragment central authority.

Libertarian skepticism of central control and high local trust favor decentralization, but low openness and conventionalism limit strong endorsement.

High trust in central government (91.7) and strong national identification (civic nationalism 5.47 is low but national identification 14.84 is moderate) produce mild opposition to decentralization, favoring unified authority.
